title: Medieval England: Magna Carta and the Black Death
subject: history
courseDescription: Power struggles between kings and barons, Magna Carta, the Black Death and the Peasants' Revolt in medieval England.
lesson:
Medieval England: Magna Carta and the Black Death

Key facts
Level: GCSE
Theme: British History
Period: 1215 — 1381

Key question. How did royal power, disease and rebellion reshape medieval English society?

In 1215, rebel barons forced King John to seal Magna Carta, limiting the king's power. In 1348 the Black Death killed a third or more of the people, shaking the feudal system. In 1381 the Peasants' Revolt demanded freedom and an end to the Poll Tax.

1 · Magna Carta and royal power

King John was unpopular: he lost lands in France, taxed heavily and quarrelled with the Church. In 1215 his barons forced him to seal Magna Carta at Runnymede. Its key idea: even the king is not above the law.

Term · Magna Carta
The 'Great Charter' of 1215. It said the king could not tax or imprison people freely and had to obey the law. It became a lasting symbol of rights and liberty.

2 · The Black Death

From 1348, the plague killed perhaps a third to a half of England's population. People blamed God's punishment or 'bad air', not germs. With so many workers dead, survivors could demand higher wages and more freedom — weakening feudal ties.

3 · The Peasants' Revolt of 1381

A hated Poll Tax — the same charge for rich and poor — sparked the Peasants' Revolt. Led by Wat Tyler, rebels marched on London demanding an end to serfdom. The young king Richard II made promises, then broke them, but the Poll Tax was dropped.

In summary

Timeline

DateEvent
1215King John seals Magna Carta
1348Black Death reaches England
1349-50Huge population loss
1381Peasants' Revolt (Wat Tyler)

Key terms

TermMeaning
Magna CartaCharter limiting the king (1215)
Black DeathPlague of 1348 onwards
Serf (villein)Unfree peasant tied to the land
Poll TaxFlat tax charged to everyone
Feudal systemRanked society, land for service

People

NameRole
King JohnSealed Magna Carta in 1215
Wat TylerLed the Peasants' Revolt
Richard IIYoung king in 1381
Watch out
Don't confuse the two changes: Magna Carta was a legal/political limit on the king, while the Black Death was a social and economic shock. And the Peasants' Revolt was 'crushed' — yet it still forced the Poll Tax to be dropped.

Charter, plague and revolt: three very different forces chipped away at the power of kings and lords. Together they began England's long journey away from rigid feudalism.

cards.recto: Which unpopular king was forced by his barons to seal Magna Carta in 1215?
cards.explanation: John lost lands in France, raised heavy taxes and quarrelled with the Church. Rebel barons forced him to accept limits on his power.

cards.recto: What document, sealed at Runnymede in 1215, limited the power of the king?
cards.explanation: Latin for 'Great Charter', it said the king was not above the law and could not tax or imprison people freely. It became a symbol of rights and liberty.

cards.recto: What key principle did Magna Carta establish about the king and the law?
cards.verso: The king is not above the law
cards.explanation: Magna Carta said even the king had to obey the law and could not act as he pleased. This idea underpins later constitutional government.

easy: The king can do whatever he likes
medium: Barons must obey the king absolutely
hard: The king appoints all bishops freely
Trials are decided by the king alone

cards.recto: Which deadly disease swept through England from 1348, killing a huge share of the population?
cards.explanation: The plague arrived in 1348 and killed perhaps a third to half of England's people within a few years, transforming society and the economy.

cards.recto: Roughly what share of England's population died in the Black Death?
cards.verso: About a third to a half
cards.explanation: Estimates suggest 30-50% of people died. This massive loss of life caused huge social and economic upheaval.

cards.recto: [Watch out] Did medieval people understand the real cause of the Black Death?
cards.verso: No; they blamed God's punishment or 'bad air', not germs
cards.explanation: A common error is to assume they knew about germs. With no germ theory, people blamed sin, God's anger or 'miasma'. Some flagellants whipped themselves to beg forgiveness.

easy: Yes, they knew fleas and bacteria caused it
medium: Yes, they blamed dirty water and germs
No, they thought it was harmless
hard: Yes, they proved it came from rats scientifically
Yes, they isolated the plague bacterium
No, because there was no disease at all

cards.recto: How did the shortage of workers after the Black Death affect peasants?
cards.verso: Survivors could demand higher wages and more freedom
cards.explanation: With so many dead, labour was scarce. Peasants could bargain for better pay and conditions, weakening the old feudal ties.

medium: Peasants were all freed by the king
Lords gained more control than ever
hard: Wages were frozen and never rose

cards.recto: What 1381 uprising was sparked by a hated Poll Tax and demands for freedom?
cards.explanation: Angry at the Poll Tax and lingering feudal duties, rebels marched on London led by Wat Tyler. It was crushed, but the Poll Tax was dropped.

cards.recto: Who was the leader of the Peasants' Revolt of 1381?
cards.explanation: Wat Tyler led the rebels into London to demand an end to serfdom and the Poll Tax. He was killed when meeting the young king Richard II.

cards.recto: Which tax, charged the same amount to everyone, helped spark the Peasants' Revolt?
cards.explanation: A flat tax per person hit the poor hardest, since everyone paid the same regardless of wealth. It caused deep anger in 1381.

cards.recto: [Compare] How did the effects of Magna Carta and the Black Death differ?
cards.verso: Magna Carta limited royal power in law; the Black Death changed society and the economy through mass death
cards.explanation: Magna Carta was a political and legal change (limits on the king). The Black Death was a social and economic shock (labour shortages, higher wages) with no law behind it.

easy: They were exactly the same kind of event
medium: Magna Carta was a plague; the Black Death was a law
hard: The Black Death was a royal charter

cards.recto: What was the strict social hierarchy of medieval England, with peasants at the bottom, called?
cards.explanation: Society was ranked from the king down through lords and knights to peasants, who worked the land in return for protection.

cards.recto: What was an unfree peasant, tied to a lord's land, called in medieval England?
cards.explanation: Serfs were bound to the land and owed work and dues to their lord. They could not leave freely. The Black Death helped erode serfdom.

cards.recto: Which powerful institution dominated religion and learning in medieval England?
cards.explanation: The Church owned land, collected tithes, ran education and shaped daily life. It answered to the Pope in Rome, not the English king alone.

cards.recto: Which young king faced the rebels of 1381 and made promises he later broke?
cards.explanation: Just 14, Richard II met the rebels, promised to end serfdom, then went back on his word once the revolt collapsed. Serfdom slowly declined anyway.

cards.recto: Overall, why is medieval England important for the story of rights and freedom?
cards.verso: Magna Carta limited the king, and events like the Black Death and the revolt weakened feudal control
cards.explanation: Together, legal limits on the king and the breakdown of serfdom began a slow shift away from absolute royal power and rigid feudalism.

easy: It made the king all-powerful forever
It had no effect on later England
medium: It created full democracy at once
hard: It gave the Pope control of England
It removed all limits on the crown
